title: Rum baba
subtitle: Yeast-raised cylinder soaked in rum syrup with whipped cream
excerpt: A rum baba is a small, cylindrical yeast cake, dry and almost bread-like when it comes out of the oven, and transformed by an extended soak in warm rum syrup. The syrup penetrates fully and the cake swells visibly as it absorbs. Serve with lightly whipped cream and, if you have it, a spoonful of jam or fresh fruit. The batter comes together quickly; the soaking is the technique.
sourceNotes: The baba au rhum was popularised in Paris by Nicolas Stohrer, pâtissier to King Stanisław Leszczyński, in the eighteenth century. Earlier versions without rum appear in Polish baking as baba; enriched Easter breads soaked in syrup. The recipe appears in Escoffier, 'Le Guide Culinaire' (1903), Project Gutenberg. The small cylindrical baba mousseline mould is the modern form; larger savarin moulds produce the savarin variant.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unsoaked babas. Soak from room temperature — allow a little longer for the syrup to penetrate.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Unsoaked babas keep for 2 days in an airtight container. Soak within 30 minutes of serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: A sugar syrup made to soak a baked item — a baba, a savarin, or a sponge layer — to add moisture and flavour. The syrup should be warm, not hot, when applied; hot syrup can break the structure. The ratio of sugar to water varies by application: a light syrup (1:4) moistens; a heavier syrup (1:2) saturates and preserves. Babas require the heavier ratio, applied over several minutes until the cake stops absorbing.

content.text: The batter is a sticky, almost pourable yeast dough; it is beaten rather than kneaded. The eggs go in one at a time, then the softened butter in small pieces. The mixture looks wrong at almost every stage. It comes right.
. It needs to be warm (not boiling) so the baba can absorb it without the alcohol evaporating. Spoon it over slowly and repeatedly; each spoonful is absorbed before the next goes on.

Put 200 g flour, 7 g yeast, 20 g sugar, and 3 g salt in a large bowl. Add the 3 beaten eggs and 40 ml warm milk. Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon or by hand for 4 to 5 minutes until the batter becomes smooth and elastic and pulls away from the sides. Add 80 g softened butter in four additions, beating well after each. The batter will be sticky and glossy. Cover and leave at room temperature for 60 minutes until doubled.

Butter 8 individual dariole moulds or a 12-hole deep muffin tin. Fill each a third full; the babas will rise to fill the mould. Cover and prove for 25 to 35 minutes until the batter reaches the top of each mould.
Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Bake for 15 to 18 minutes until deep golden on top and a skewer comes out clean. Turn out immediately onto a rack and leave to cool for 10 minutes.

Bring 300 ml water and 200 g sugar to a simmer, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Remove from the heat and add 100 ml dark rum and the zest of 1 lemon. Let the syrup cool to warm; about 60 degrees C. Place the babas in a shallow dish and spoon the warm syrup over them repeatedly, turning them, until they stop absorbing and look swollen and shiny. This takes 5 to 8 minutes per baba. They can be left in the syrup for up to 30 minutes before serving.
Serve with lightly whipped cream alongside.

items.problem: Babas are dry and not absorbing the syrup
items.solution: The syrup was too cold or the babas were too fresh and their structure was still set. Warm the syrup to about 60 degrees C and pierce the top of each baba with a skewer before soaking to help the syrup penetrate.

items.problem: Babas fell in on themselves after soaking
items.solution: The batter was over-proved before baking or under-baked. The gluten structure needs to be fully set before soaking; an under-baked baba collapses under the weight of the syrup.
The moulds were filled too full. Babas should fill only one-third of the mould; a half-full mould produces a dense plug rather than a light, risen cake.
